perturbation.original_text: For a period of eighteen months following the execution of this agreement to the Receiving Party shall not, directly or indirectly hire, solicit, or encourage to leave the Disclosing Party employm ent, any employee, contractor or player of the Disclosing Party or hire any such employee or contractor who has left the Disclosing Parties employment, contractual engagement or player within one year of such employment or engagement , the Receiving Party .
perturbation.changed_text: For approximately eighteen months following the execution of this agreement, the Receiving Party should endeavor not to, directly or indirectly, hire, solicit, or encourage to leave the Disclosing Party's employment, any employee, contractor or player of the Disclosing Party, or hire any such employee or contractor who has left the Disclosing Parties employment, contractual engagement or player within approximately one year of such employment or engagement, the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'approximately eighteen months' and 'should endeavor not to' introduce ambiguity, weakening the enforceability of the non-solicitation clause. The original clause provides a clear, defined period. The modified version introduces uncertainty regarding the duration of the restriction and the level of effort required to avoid solicitation, which is contradictory to established labor laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While this NDA includes a non-solicitation clause, the ambiguity introduced makes it difficult to determine if the 'endeavor not to' language combined with 'approximately' acts as a de facto restriction on an employee's ability to work, potentially conflicting with the strong public policy against restraints of trade in California. The ambiguity challenges a clearly defined period to perform the restriction on trade, which can be seen as an anti-competitive action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'approximately eighteen months' and 'should endeavor not to,' which weakens the original non-solicitation clause. This directly conflicts with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text provided a clear, defined period, while the changed text makes the restriction on trade indefinite. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The ambiguity introduced weakens the enforceability of the original restriction, acting as a de facto restriction on trade by allowing for a less definite period of the restriction to be performed, creating a contradiction. The original text is a restraint of trade, and the adjusted text is a lesser restraint of trade, but both are still a restraint of trade according to the explanation.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of three years following the execution of this agreement , the contractor shall not start up his/her own cricket related business using the customer lists, trade secrets, inventions, innovations, processes, information, records an d specificatio ns of the Disclosing Party and/or used by the Disclosing party in connection with the operation of its business including, without limitation, the Disclosing parties business and product processes, methods, accounts and procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: For what is considered to be a reasonable period following the execution of this agreement, the contractor should refrain from starting up his/her own cricket-related business using, to a significant extent, the customer lists, trade secrets, inventions, innovations, processes, information, records and specifications of the Disclosing Party and/or used by the Disclosing party in connection with the operation of its business including, without limitation, the Disclosing parties business and product processes, methods, accounts and procedures.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'what is considered to be a reasonable period' and 'should refrain from' create ambiguity regarding the duration and the level of prohibition. The phrase 'to a significant extent' also creates ambiguity. The original clause has a clear definition of duration and action. The modified version weakens the restrictions and could violate California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. The vague language introduced makes the clause difficult to enforce, and the 'reasonable period' and 'significant extent' weaken the protection afforded to the Disclosing Party, potentially making the restriction overly broad and therefore invalid under California law. The court may find that this is a trade restriction and not a proper NDA.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'what is considered to be a reasonable period,' 'should refrain from,' and 'to a significant extent.' This weakens the original clause's clear definition of duration and action. According to the provided law snippet from California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The perturbation explanation and the law explanation suggest that the vague language could make the clause unenforceable and overly broad, potentially violating California law by creating an invalid restraint of trade. The added ambiguity directly contradicts the intent of BPC 16600.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall procure that its employees, agents and sub -contractors to whom Proprietary Information is disclosed or who have access to Proprietary Information sign a nondisclosure or similar ag reement in content substantially similar to this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will use its best efforts to encourage its employees, agents and sub-contractors to whom Proprietary Information is disclosed or who have access to Proprietary Information to sign a nondisclosure agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'shall procure' with 'will use its best efforts to encourage', the legal obligation is weakened. The original text requires the Receiving Party to ensure that employees, agents, and subcontractors sign a non-disclosure agreement. The modified text only requires the Receiving Party to 'encourage' them to sign, which is not a binding obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UTSA doesn't mandate specific NDA requirements, it provides a framework for protecting trade secrets. By weakening the requirement to ensure that employees and subcontractors are bound by NDAs, the modified clause makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to demonstrate that it took 'reasonable measures' to protect its trade secrets, as required by the UTSA for a claim of misappropriation. If the Receiving party only encourages, it could be deemed as not taking the needed 'reasonable measures'.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall procure' to 'will use its best efforts to encourage' weakens the obligation on the Receiving Party to ensure NDAs are signed. The Law Explanation states that this weakening makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to demonstrate they took 'reasonable measures' to protect their trade secrets. Although there are no specific scraped snippets, the Law Explanation highlights the need for 'reasonable measures', which is undermined by only 'encouraging' employees to sign NDAs.
